title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions

input_row.title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
input_row.description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
input_row.rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container. Impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
input_row.audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
input_row.remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault Default Value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ 2. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/
input_row.impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_security_seccomp_profile_runtime_default_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Enumerate all Pod and PodTemplateSpec resources in the cluster, including Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, ReplicaSets, Jobs, CronJobs, and standalone Pods.
- For each Pod spec (including those in higher-level controllers), inspect the `.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` field at both the Pod and container level (container-level overrides Pod-level).
- Validate that the `seccompProfile.type` is set to `RuntimeDefault` (which maps to the Docker/default seccomp profile).
- Pass Criteria: Every Pod and container in the cluster has `securityContext.seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault` (or, for legacy compatibility, `docker/default` if supported by the Kubernetes version).
- Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container is missing the `seccompProfile` field, or has it set to `Unconfined`, or any value other than `RuntimeDefault` (or `docker/default` where supported).
- Example kubectl command to enumerate and check:
  ```
  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace, pod_seccomp: .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile, container_seccomp: [.spec.containers[].securityContext.seccompProfile]}'
  ```
- For higher-level controllers (Deployments, etc.), use:
  ```
  kubectl get deployment,statefulset,daemonset,job,cronjob --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {kind: .kind, name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace, pod_seccomp: .spec.template.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile, container_seccomp: [.spec.template.spec.containers[].securityContext.seccompProfile]}'
  ```
- Ensure that all returned `seccompProfile.type` values are `RuntimeDefault`.
- This check is fully automatable via the Kubernetes API and does not require host-level access or subjective review.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11.1 PDF
